FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study (release 2.0), cross-sectional sample of 11,875 U.S. youth aged 9-11 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time measured from Youth Screen Time Survey including: TV watching, video watching (e.g., YouTube), video game playing, texting on cellphone/tablet/computer, visiting social media sites, using video chat. Calculated as weighted average of weekday and weekend screen time.

FT_IC3.evidence: Negative binomial regression examining associations of meeting different combinations of movement behavior recommendations with internalizing, externalizing, and total problems. Multiple statistical tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Prevalence ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported. Example: meeting all three recommendations PR .77 (95% CI .68-.86) for total problem scores, PR .78 (95% CI .68-.89) for internalizing, PR .79 (95% CI .68-.91) for externalizing.

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional original empirical analysis using negative binomial regression with adjusted covariates

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal of Adolescent Health, original research article

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis; screen time is a predictor (IV) of non-SM outcomes (internalizing/externalizing behaviors). Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria which requires SM as DV with only demographic predictors in longitudinal design.
notes: E2 applied: screen time extracted separately from 24-hour movement composite (screen time + sleep, vs screen time alone, vs physical activity alone)

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study baseline data, N=15,022 American children aged 9-11 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as parent-reported average weekday hours of recreational screen use (continuous variable). Described as proxy for screen time behavior.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects regression models with interaction terms (Model 1: family income + race; Model 2: added interaction of household income × race). Regression coefficients, SE, 95% CI, and p-values reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (b) with standard error, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values reported. Example: Income 50-100K: b=-0.60, p<0.001; Income >100K: b=-1.20, p<0.001; Race (Black): b=1.32, p<0.001; Interaction Income (>100K) × Race (Black): b=0.74, p=0.003

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional secondary analysis using ABCD baseline data with mixed-effects regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Information journal (MDPI), original empirical research article

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis (not longitudinal/prospective). Although screen time is the dependent variable and predictors are primarily demographic (family income, race, sociodemographic covariates), FT-EC7 explicitly requires LONGITUDINAL/PROSPECTIVE design. E14 applies instead: sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM in cross-sectional context = MET inclusion.
notes: E14 applied: Sociodemographic predictors (family income, race) of screen time in cross-sectional analysis; inclusion criteria met per rule E14.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, N=11,670 U.S. participants (baseline), N=4,951 (longitudinal subset), aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary exposures are sleep disturbance (Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children subdomains) and total sleep time. Screen time is mentioned as a covariate but is NOT a focal smartphone/SM exposure variable. No smartphone ownership, app use, social media platform use, problematic use scales, or bedtime phone/SM use measured.

FT_IC3.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT_IC2 failure

FT_IC4.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT_IC2 failure

FT_IC5.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT_IC2 failure

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal of Adolescent Health, original research article, published 2020

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure); FT_EC7 evaluation not applicable.
notes: EXCLUSION: Paper does not measure smartphone or social media as primary exposure. Focus is sleep disturbance → depression. Screen time included as covariate only, not analyzed separately. Fails FT_IC2 criterion.
